perturbation.original_text: 1.2.8 “Client ” means Client Name, a company incorporated in terms of the laws of South Africa which provides ………………..
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2.8 “Client ” means Client Name, an entity providing services in South Africa.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Client' from 'a company incorporated in terms of the laws of South Africa' to 'an entity providing services in South Africa' broadens the definition. This could include partnerships, sole proprietorships, or even international companies. This contradicts section 14, which refers to 'Client Brand' and assumes that the Client entity is structured in a way to have a 'brand'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 14 STIPULATIO ALTERI To the extent that any provisions of this Agreement purport to be for the benefit of a Client Brand, the Parties confirm that such provisions are intended to be a stipulatio alteri (i.e. a contract for the benefit of a third part y) capable of acceptance at any time by such Client Brand. Such acceptance may only take place by way of a written notice to that effect given to AfriGIS by Client on behalf of the Client Brand in question.
perturbation.justification: The change broadens the definition of 'Client' from a specific type of entity ('a company incorporated in terms of the laws of South Africa') to a more general 'entity providing services in South Africa.' This contradicts section 14, which references 'Client Brand' and assumes a certain level of corporate structure to have a brand. The broader definition could include entities without a brand, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party agrees that it will, during or after the course of their relationship and/or the term of this Agreement as described in clause 9, keep the Confidential Information in the strictest confidence and will not disclose it to any third party for any reason or purpose whatsoever without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, save in accordance with t he provisions of this Agreement, and the Parties undertake to each other that their holding and subsidiary companies or agents shall be bound by the provisions of this Agreement. For avoidance of doubt, in this Agreement “third party” means any party other than Client and AfriGIS and their holding and subsidiary companies or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party agrees that it will, during or after the course of their relationship and/or the term of this Agreement as described in clause 9, keep the Confidential Information secure and protected, and will not disclose it to unauthorized individuals. For avoidance of doubt, in this Agreement “unauthorized individuals” means any individual who has not completed a confidentiality training course.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'third party' explicitly. By replacing the obligation not to disclose to a 'third party' with an obligation not to disclose to 'unauthorized individuals', and redefining 'unauthorized individuals', this creates a contradiction. Initially, a 'third party' meant anyone outside AfriGIS and the Client, including their related entities. The change limits the scope to only individuals who have not completed a confidentiality training course. This contradicts the broader protection intended by the original definition, especially regarding subsidiary companies and agents.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party agrees that it will, during or after the course of their relationship and/or the term of this Agreement as described in clause 9, keep the Confidential Information secure and protected, and will not disclose it to unauthorized individuals. For avoidance of doubt, in this Agreement “unauthorized individuals” means any individual who has not completed a confidentiality training course.
perturbation.justification: The original text broadly defined 'third party' to include any party outside of the Client and AfriGIS, including their holding and subsidiary companies or agents. The changed text narrows the scope by replacing 'third party' with 'unauthorized individuals' and defining 'unauthorized individuals' as those who haven't completed a confidentiality training course. This creates a direct contradiction as it allows for disclosure to entities like subsidiary companies and agents, provided they have completed the training, whereas the original text forbade any disclosure to these entities without prior written consent. This contradicts the previously defined broader protection, making enforcement unclear and thus establishing a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5 RESTRICTIONS ON DISCLOSURE AND USE OF TH E CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION The Receiving Party undertake s not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than – 5.1 that for which it is disclosed; 5.2 in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement; and 5.3 in accordance with applicable data privacy legislation, as amended from time to time.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 RESTRICTIONS ON DISCLOSURE AND USE OF THE DATA The Receiving Party undertakes not to use the Data for any purpose other than – 5.1 that for which it is disclosed; 5.2 in accordance with the provisions of this Agreement; and 5.3 in accordance with applicable industry best practices.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Data', and 'applicable data privacy legislation' with 'applicable industry best practices' creates a contradiction. Section 1.2.2 defines 'Confidential Information'. This change makes section 5 only apply to the new term 'Data'. The contract is no longer enforcing any obligation to comply with any data privacy legislation, in the use of 'Data'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.2.2 “Confidential Information” means any confidential information, documentation or data of whatever nature relating to a Party or its subsidiaries which may have been or which may be obtained by or disclosed to the other Party during the course of its relationship with such Party, whether in writing, in electronic form or pursuant to discussions, including without limitation: trade secrets, know -how, marketing and advertising strategies, strategic objectives, planning or ideas, research, business activities, business relationships, products or proposed products, proposals, pricing details, strategies, customer and client details, schematics, software, computer programmes and technology, operating procedures and methodologies, designs, drawings, functional and technical requirements and specifications and any other technical, business, financial or market information or any other information which may reasonably be regarded as being confidential and of a proprietary nature to such Party or any of its subsidiaries or holding companies and;
perturbation.justification: The original text used 'Confidential Information' and 'applicable data privacy legislation,' aligning with the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1.2.2. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Data' and 'applicable data privacy legislation' with 'applicable industry best practices.' This creates a contradiction because Section 5 no longer applies to 'Confidential Information' as defined in 1.2.2, meaning the contract no longer enforces obligations relating to data privacy legislation in the use of 'Confidential Information', which contradicts the intention of the original text and creates misalignment.

perturbation.original_text: 7 RETURN OF MATERIAL CONTAINING OR PERTAINING TO THE CONFIDENT IAL INFORMATION 7.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving P arty to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to the Confidential Information and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has not retained i n its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.
perturbation.changed_text: 7 RETURN OF MATERIAL CONTAINING OR PERTAINING TO THE DOCUMENTS 7.1 The Disclosing Party may, at any time, request the Receiving P arty to return any material containing, pertaining to or relating to the Documents and may, in addition request the Receiving Party to furnish a written statement to the effect that, upon such return, the Receiving Party has not retained i n its possession, or under its control, either directly or indirectly, any such material.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Documents'. 'Confidential Information' is defined in Section 1.2.2. 'Documents' is never defined. This creates ambiguity over which material is obligated to be returned. It is unclear if it refers to all documents or only those containing 'Confidential Information' and contradicts the general intention of the NDA.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.2.2 “Confidential Information” means any confidential information, documentation or data of whatever nature relating to a Party or its subsidiaries which may have been or which may be obtained by or disclosed to the other Party during the course of its relationship with such Party, whether in writing, in electronic form or pursuant to discussions, including without limitation: trade secrets, know -how, marketing and advertising strategies, strategic objectives, planning or ideas, research, business activities, business relationships, products or proposed products, proposals, pricing details, strategies, customer and client details, schematics, software, computer programmes and technology, operating procedures and methodologies, designs, drawings, functional and technical requirements and specifications and any other technical, business, financial or market information or any other information which may reasonably be regarded as being confidential and of a proprietary nature to such Party or any of its subsidiaries or holding companies and;
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information', which is specifically defined in section 1.2.2. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Documents', which is not defined anywhere in the contract. This creates a contradiction because the obligation to return material is now ambiguous; it is unclear whether the receiving party must return all documents, or only those containing information that would have previously fallen under the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Because 'Documents' could be interpreted much more broadly than 'Confidential Information' the receiving party's obligation becomes less clear, a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 16 NOTICES AND DOMICILIUM 16.1 The Parties hereto choose domicilium citandi et executandi for all purposes of and in connection with this Agree ment as follows – 16.1.1 AfriGIS : - JV Communicatio For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical add ress: Rigel Park Block A 446 Rigel Avenue South Erasmusrand Email: legal@afrigis.co.za 16.1.2 Client : For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical address: ………………………….. ………………………….. ………………………… Fax /Email: ………………………….
perturbation.changed_text: 16 NOTICES AND DOMICILIUM 16.1 The Parties hereto choose correspondence address for all purposes of and in connection with this Agree ment as follows – 16.1.1 AfriGIS : - JV Communicatio For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical add ress: Rigel Park Block A 446 Rigel Avenue South Erasmusrand Email: legal@afrigis.co.za 16.1.2 Client : For the attention of: The Managing Director Physical address: ………………………….. ………………………….. ………………………… Fax /Email: ………………………….
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'domicilium citandi et executandi' to 'correspondence address' removes the legal implication. 'Domicilium citandi et executandi' means the address for legal notices and proceedings. By changing this, you create a contradiction: the section still details a physical address, implying legal service, but the heading suggests it is just a place to send normal mail. This would create uncertainty about whether legal proceedings are validly served if sent to that address, making section 16.3 contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16.3 All notices, demands, legal proceedings, communications or pay ments intended for any Party shall be made or given at such Party’s domicilium for the time being.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies 'domicilium citandi et executandi', which is a legal term indicating the address for legal notices. The changed text replaces this with 'correspondence address', a general term for mail. Section 16.3 requires that all legal notices be sent to the Party's 'domicilium'. By changing the address designation from 'domicilium' to 'correspondence address', it creates uncertainty whether serving legal notices at that address is valid. Thus the changed text creates a contradiction with Section 16.3 because Section 16.3 still requires legal notices to be sent to the 'domicilium' and the modified text now refers to the addresses as a 'correspondence address', implying they are not the same and thus creating a contradiction.
